subtopic_detail: Aldosterone-renin ratio screening in resistant hypertension
stem: A 46-year-old has stage 2 hypertension uncontrolled despite three antihypertensives at full dose: perindopril 8 mg, indapamide 2.5 mg, and amlodipine 10 mg. Home BP averages 158/96. Potassium is 3.2 mmol/L without a diuretic dose change. He has no kidney disease, no obstructive sleep apnoea, and no use of liquorice or nasal decongestants. The endocrinologist plans to investigate for primary hyperaldosteronism.

data_table.Examination: BMI 31, no Cushingoid features, no abdominal bruit, normal fundi
data_table.Investigations: Na 142, K 3.2, eGFR 82, plasma renin and aldosterone not yet drawn, urine albumin-creatinine ratio 2.5 mg/mmol

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate preparation before drawing the aldosterone-renin ratio?

options.text: Continue all current antihypertensive agents unchanged and draw the aldosterone-renin ratio in the morning after 8 hours of overnight recumbency
options.rationale: Some antihypertensives substantially alter the aldosterone-renin ratio and must be adjusted before testing. (Source: Endocrine Society Guideline: Primary Aldosteronism)

options.text: Stop all antihypertensive medications for 4 weeks and treat hypertension only with hydralazine plus metoprolol during the washout period before testing
options.rationale: Beta-blockers also suppress renin and increase the aldosterone-renin ratio, generating false positives. (Source: Endocrine Society Guideline: Primary Aldosteronism)

options.text: Replace potassium above 4.0 mmol/L, stop any mineralocorticoid antagonist for 4 weeks, and substitute prazosin, hydralazine, moxonidine, or verapamil for sampling
options.rationale: Mineralocorticoid receptor antagonists must be stopped 4 weeks before sampling; potassium must be replete; non-interfering agents like prazosin, hydralazine, moxonidine, and verapamil can manage hypertension during testing. (Source: Endocrine Society Guideline: Primary Aldosteronism)

options.text: Draw aldosterone-renin ratio in the supine position at midnight to maximise diagnostic sensitivity and avoid morning circadian effects
options.rationale: Sampling is done after 2 hours of upright posture in the morning, not in the supine position at midnight. (Source: Endocrine Society Guideline: Primary Aldosteronism)

options.text: Restrict dietary sodium to under 80 mmol daily for 2 weeks before sampling to suppress aldosterone secretion and enhance test specificity
options.rationale: Sodium restriction alters the renin-aldosterone axis and is not part of routine screening preparation. (Source: Endocrine Society Guideline: Primary Aldosteronism)

explanation.summary: Primary aldosteronism is a common cause of resistant hypertension, particularly with spontaneous hypokalaemia. Screening uses the aldosterone-renin ratio after potassium repletion and washout of interfering medications. Mineralocorticoid receptor antagonists, eplerenone, and amiloride must be stopped 4 weeks beforehand. Beta-blockers reduce renin and increase the ratio falsely. ACE inhibitors, angiotensin receptor blockers, and dihydropyridines may be continued with caution. Sampling is done in the morning after 2 hours upright.
key_points: Stop mineralocorticoid receptor antagonists for 4 weeks before testing
Verapamil, prazosin, hydralazine, and moxonidine do not interfere with screening
explanation.pearls: Confirmatory testing with saline loading or fludrocortisone suppression follows a positive screen; do not commit to surgery on a screen alone.

subtopic_detail: Adrenal crisis recognition and emergency management
stem: A 38-year-old with primary adrenal insufficiency on hydrocortisone 20 mg and fludrocortisone 0.1 mg daily presents to the emergency department after 36 hours of vomiting and diarrhoea from gastroenteritis. She has been unable to keep down her morning hydrocortisone. She feels dizzy on standing and has had no urine output today.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 124, RR 22, T 37.1, BP 86/52 lying, SpO2 99%
data_table.Examination: Dry mucous membranes, cool peripheries, capillary refill 4 seconds, no focal abdominal signs
data_table.Investigations: Na 128, K 5.6, glucose 3.8, creatinine 132, urea 14, venous blood gas pH 7.31, bicarbonate 18, lactate 2.6

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate first action?

options.text: Administer oral hydrocortisone 40 mg now and admit to the short-stay unit for observation and oral rehydration over 6 hours
options.rationale: Persistent vomiting precludes oral absorption; parenteral hydrocortisone is required without delay in adrenal crisis. (Source: ESA: Adrenal Insufficiency Guidelines)

options.text: Administer intravenous hydrocortisone 100 mg and 1 L of sodium chloride 0.9% over 1 hour, then admit for ongoing hydrocortisone 200 mg over 24 hours
options.rationale: Adrenal crisis requires immediate intravenous hydrocortisone 100 mg bolus followed by infusion or repeated doses to total 200 mg over 24 hours, plus aggressive intravenous fluid resuscitation. (Source: ESA: Adrenal Insufficiency Guidelines)

options.text: Administer intravenous dexamethasone 8 mg and 500 mL of 5% dextrose over 1 hour as first-line treatment for adrenal crisis
options.rationale: Hydrocortisone is preferred because it provides mineralocorticoid as well as glucocorticoid coverage; dexamethasone is used only when retaining diagnostic capacity matters. (Source: ESA: Adrenal Insufficiency Guidelines)

options.text: Administer oral fludrocortisone 0.2 mg and intravenous 5% dextrose at 100 mL/hour to correct hypoglycaemia and electrolyte imbalance
options.rationale: Fludrocortisone is not needed acutely because high-dose hydrocortisone has sufficient mineralocorticoid activity at 100 mg or more daily. (Source: ESA: Adrenal Insufficiency Guidelines)

options.text: Defer steroid until plasma cortisol level returns within 2 hours and initiate intravenous antibiotics empirically before any hormonal intervention
options.rationale: Empirical steroids are given immediately in suspected adrenal crisis without waiting for cortisol level results; antibiotics are added if sepsis is suspected. (Source: ESA: Adrenal Insufficiency Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Adrenal crisis is a medical emergency requiring immediate intravenous hydrocortisone and aggressive fluid resuscitation. Standard dosing is hydrocortisone 100 mg intravenous bolus followed by 200 mg over 24 hours by infusion or 50 mg every 6 hours. Hydrocortisone provides sufficient mineralocorticoid effect at these doses, so fludrocortisone is not required acutely. Glucose monitoring and treatment of hypoglycaemia are essential. Investigation for and treatment of precipitating infection occurs simultaneously.
key_points: Hydrocortisone 100 mg intravenous followed by 200 mg over 24 hours in adrenal crisis
Fluid resuscitate with sodium chloride 0.9% and treat hypoglycaemia
Identify and treat precipitating illness in parallel
explanation.pearls: All patients with adrenal insufficiency should have hydrocortisone for self-injection at home and stress-dose instructions; an Australian endocrine emergency wristband or card aids identification.

stem: A 41-year-old has biochemically confirmed phaeochromocytoma with plasma metanephrines 12-fold elevated and a 5 cm right adrenal mass with avid uptake on MIBG. He is referred to the endocrine team for preoperative optimisation. He has paroxysmal palpitations, headaches, and pallor 3 to 4 times weekly. His BP swings between 165/96 during episodes and 138/78 at rest. He has no other comorbidity.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 96 sinus rhythm, BP 152/92 at rest, no orthostatic drop yet
data_table.Examination: BMI 22, no abdominal mass palpable, no carotid bruit
data_table.Investigations: Plasma metanephrines elevated; MIBG positive at the right adrenal; CT confirms 5 cm right adrenal mass; ECG sinus rhythm, no left ventricular hypertrophy

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate preoperative blockade plan?

options.text: Start metoprolol 25 mg twice daily today and titrate to heart rate under 80 over 2 weeks before initiating alpha blockade
options.rationale: Beta blockade before alpha blockade unopposes alpha-mediated vasoconstriction and can precipitate hypertensive crisis. (Source: ESA: Phaeochromocytoma Guideline)

options.text: Start oral amlodipine 5 mg daily and refer for elective adrenalectomy in 5 to 7 days without preoperative alpha or beta blockade
options.rationale: Calcium channel blockade alone is inadequate preoperative blockade; alpha blockade is mandatory. (Source: ESA: Phaeochromocytoma Guideline)

options.text: Recommend immediate adrenalectomy without preoperative blockade because anaesthetic alpha and beta agents can be titrated intraoperatively
options.rationale: Preoperative blockade for 10 to 14 days reduces intraoperative haemodynamic instability and is mandatory. (Source: ESA: Phaeochromocytoma Guideline)

options.text: Start phenoxybenzamine 10 mg twice daily, titrate with high-sodium diet, then add beta-blocker if reflex tachycardia develops, with surgery at 10 to 14 days
options.rationale: Australian and international practice is alpha blockade first, typically phenoxybenzamine, with high-salt diet to expand volume; beta blockade added only if reflex tachycardia, then adrenalectomy at 10 to 14 days. (Source: ESA: Phaeochromocytoma Guideline)

options.text: Start labetalol 100 mg twice daily as a single agent for combined alpha and beta blockade to streamline preoperative preparation
options.rationale: Labetalol has greater beta than alpha activity and can produce paradoxical hypertension in phaeochromocytoma. (Source: ESA: Phaeochromocytoma Guideline)

explanation.summary: Preoperative blockade in phaeochromocytoma must be alpha first, beta second, to prevent unopposed alpha-mediated vasoconstriction and hypertensive crisis. Phenoxybenzamine is the most common alpha blocker; doxazosin is an alternative when phenoxybenzamine causes troublesome side effects. A high-sodium diet and increased fluid intake expand intravascular volume. Beta blockade is added only after adequate alpha blockade if reflex tachycardia develops. Surgery is generally performed at 10 to 14 days when targets are met.
key_points: Alpha first, beta second for phaeochromocytoma preoperative blockade
Phenoxybenzamine is the standard alpha blocker; doxazosin is an alternative
High-sodium diet and volume expansion are essential adjuncts
explanation.pearls: Labetalol is contraindicated as monotherapy in phaeochromocytoma because the relative beta blockade overshadows the alpha component and can precipitate crisis.

subtopic_detail: Massive pulmonary embolism with haemodynamic compromise
stem: A 62-year-old presents collapsed 90 minutes after returning from a 24-hour flight. She remains haemodynamically unstable on arrival. She has no prior bleeding history, no recent surgery, no head injury, no current anticoagulation, and no known coagulopathy. She is alert and able to consent. The on-call respiratory and cardiology teams have reviewed and agree she has massive pulmonary embolism.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 132, RR 32, T 36.9, BP 78/48, SpO2 86% on 15 L/min non-rebreather mask
data_table.Examination: Cool peripheries, raised jugular venous pressure, prominent right ventricular heave, no audible third heart sound, no peripheral oedema
data_table.Investigations: Bedside echocardiogram shows severe right ventricular dilatation with septal flattening; troponin elevated; CT pulmonary angiogram shows bilateral central pulmonary emboli; lactate 4.2

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate immediate next step?

options.text: Administer intravenous alteplase 100 mg over 2 hours then commence unfractionated heparin once activated partial thromboplastin time normalises
options.rationale: Massive pulmonary embolism with sustained hypotension is a class I indication for systemic thrombolysis, typically alteplase 100 mg over 2 hours, followed by anticoagulation. (Source: Thoracic Society of Australia and New Zealand: Pulmonary Embolism)
source_refs: Thoracic Society of Australia and New Zealand: Pulmonary Embolism

options.text: Initiate therapeutic enoxaparin 1.5 mg/kg subcutaneously twice daily and admit to the medical assessment unit for monitoring with cardiology review tomorrow
options.rationale: Therapeutic anticoagulation alone is inadequate for haemodynamically unstable massive pulmonary embolism; reperfusion is required. (Source: Thoracic Society of Australia and New Zealand: Pulmonary Embolism)
source_refs: Thoracic Society of Australia and New Zealand: Pulmonary Embolism

options.text: Insert an inferior vena cava filter immediately as definitive therapy without anticoagulation or reperfusion treatment
options.rationale: Inferior vena cava filters are reserved for those with contraindications to anticoagulation or recurrent emboli despite therapy. (Source: Thoracic Society of Australia and New Zealand: Pulmonary Embolism)
source_refs: Thoracic Society of Australia and New Zealand: Pulmonary Embolism

options.text: Commence non-invasive ventilation with bilevel positive airway pressure and reassess for thrombolysis if not improving after 4 hours of trial
options.rationale: Non-invasive ventilation does not address the haemodynamic obstruction; delay in reperfusion increases mortality. (Source: Thoracic Society of Australia and New Zealand: Pulmonary Embolism)
source_refs: Thoracic Society of Australia and New Zealand: Pulmonary Embolism

options.text: Refer to interventional radiology for outpatient catheter-directed thrombolysis within the next 7 to 10 days after stabilisation on heparin alone
options.rationale: Catheter-directed thrombolysis or surgical embolectomy is considered for those with absolute contraindications to systemic thrombolysis; delay of 7 days is unacceptable in massive pulmonary embolism. (Source: Thoracic Society of Australia and New Zealand: Pulmonary Embolism)
source_refs: Thoracic Society of Australia and New Zealand: Pulmonary Embolism

explanation.summary: Massive or high-risk pulmonary embolism is defined by sustained hypotension and is a class I indication for systemic thrombolysis. Standard dosing is alteplase 100 mg intravenously over 2 hours followed by unfractionated heparin once activated partial thromboplastin time is approaching normal. Surgical embolectomy or catheter-directed therapy is reserved for those with absolute contraindications to systemic thrombolysis. Intermediate-high risk pulmonary embolism with normotension but right ventricular strain may benefit from selective rescue thrombolysis if deterioration occurs.
key_points: Massive pulmonary embolism with hypotension warrants systemic thrombolysis
Alteplase 100 mg over 2 hours then heparin is standard dosing
Surgical embolectomy or catheter-directed therapy is reserved for thrombolysis contraindications
explanation.pearls: Even after thrombolysis many patients have persistent right ventricular strain; follow-up echocardiography at 3 to 6 months screens for chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension.

sources.label: Thoracic Society of Australia and New Zealand: Pulmonary Embolism

subtopic_detail: Suspected osteomyelitis underlying a diabetic foot ulcer
stem: A 64-year-old has had a chronic 2 cm plantar ulcer over the first metatarsal head for 8 weeks with surrounding cellulitis and ongoing serous discharge. He has type 2 diabetes for 18 years, HbA1c 8.6, peripheral neuropathy, and palpable pulses. Probe-to-bone with a sterile probe is positive today through the ulcer. There is no acute necrosis.

data_table.Examination: 2 cm plantar ulcer over first metatarsal head with sloughy base; positive probe-to-bone; surrounding 3 cm cellulitis without crepitus; ankle-brachial index 1.0; sensation to 10 g monofilament absent in forefoot
data_table.Investigations: WCC 11.8; CRP 56; ESR 78; HbA1c 8.6; foot x-ray shows cortical erosion of first metatarsal head; MRI not yet performed; previous wound swab grew Staphylococcus aureus and Streptococcus agalactiae

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Continue outpatient oral cephalexin 500 mg four times daily for a further 4 weeks and review at the diabetic foot clinic in 6 weeks
options.rationale: Outpatient cephalexin is inadequate for suspected osteomyelitis; bone-penetrating broader regimens are required after appropriate sampling. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: diabetic foot)

options.text: Order an outpatient bone biopsy in 8 weeks while continuing topical wound dressings and routine podiatry review every 2 weeks
options.rationale: Delay of 8 weeks in suspected osteomyelitis risks progression and amputation; admission and timely sampling are warranted. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: diabetic foot)

options.text: Treat empirically with oral metronidazole 400 mg three times daily as monotherapy for 6 weeks and review at the wound clinic monthly
options.rationale: Metronidazole alone does not cover Staphylococcus aureus or aerobic streptococci, which are the dominant pathogens in diabetic foot osteomyelitis. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: diabetic foot)

options.text: Proceed directly to mid-foot amputation today without further investigation or culture-directed antimicrobial therapy as definitive treatment
options.rationale: Surgical resection of infected bone is selective; amputation is reserved for failed medical therapy, extensive necrosis, or limb-threatening sepsis. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: diabetic foot)

options.text: Admit for orthopaedic and infectious diseases input, bone biopsy with histology and culture, and 6 weeks of culture-directed intravenous then oral antibiotic therapy
options.rationale: Positive probe-to-bone with cortical erosion is highly predictive of osteomyelitis; targeted bone biopsy guides 6 weeks of culture-directed antibiotic therapy with surgical input as needed. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: diabetic foot)

explanation.summary: A positive probe-to-bone in a chronic diabetic foot ulcer with raised inflammatory markers and cortical erosion on x-ray is highly predictive of osteomyelitis. Optimal management combines admission for multidisciplinary input including orthopaedic, infectious diseases, vascular, podiatry, and diabetes care. Bone biopsy with histology and culture guides 6 weeks of intravenous followed by oral culture-directed antibiotic therapy. Surgical resection of infected bone is considered when medical therapy fails, when there is extensive necrosis, or when sepsis threatens limb viability.
key_points: Probe-to-bone plus cortical erosion strongly predicts diabetic foot osteomyelitis
Bone biopsy with culture guides 6-week antibiotic therapy
explanation.pearls: Vascular assessment with ankle-brachial index and toe pressures is essential before antibiotic-only management because ischaemia mandates revascularisation.

sources.label: Diabetes Feet Australia: Diabetic Foot Guidelines
